title: Chapter Five: Recognition
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

"Kill him!"

"Crazy bastard!"

The remaining men surged forward, swords drawn, faces contorted with rage. Yeon-shin did not look at them. He kept his eyes on the Blade Sect Leader, his sword still buried in the dead man's back, his foot planted on the corpse, his chin lifted.

*Whether I die now or in five years.*

It was the day he had learned he was dying. Twenty at most. Less than five more. Something in him had broken -- not his courage or his will. The thing that makes tomorrow seem certain. That was gone.

*If I am going to die, I will die standing.*

"Stop."

The voice did not need to be raised. The charging men froze.

"Your spirit," the Blade Sect Leader said.

He was looking at Yeon-shin. Not the bodies. The boy.

"It surpasses everyone in this estate combined."

Silence.

"Just kill him!" A red-haired warrior -- Blood Flame Sect. "Even if he's from the Desolate Sect, they can't question a bloodied corpse!"

Yeon-shin did not look at the man. He looked at the Blade Sect Leader.

"Steward!" he called.

In the doorway of the reception hall, Bak flinched. The steward staggered upright, clutching his side where blood seeped through his fingers.

"Y-yes, Young Master."

"You said you were building an intelligence network covering Xinye," Yeon-shin said. His voice was level. "These arrogant fools tried to massacre the estate with just over a dozen men, without even setting up a siege. Surely the ears and mouths of the Jeong estate reach at least as far as Xiangyang?"

"Xiangyang... Xiangyang is within reach."

Yeon-shin looked back at the Blade Sect Leader.

The bluff was simple. Xiangyang was close. A runner could reach the Desolate Sect's nearest outpost in hours. And the Desolate Sect was not gentle in its responses.

*I have no idea if anyone was sent. Father never told me anything.*

But the Blade Sect Leader could not know that.

"If you can't kill me," Yeon-shin said, and he pulled his sword from the dead man's back in a single smooth motion, the blade trailing a long ribbon of blood, "then don't make any more moves."

The Blood Flame warrior screamed.

"We've already incurred the grudge! How can we not kill him when it's obvious —"

His head exploded.

No one saw the attack. One moment the man was shouting; the next his skull burst apart and his body crumpled.

The Blade Sect Leader lowered his hand. He had not drawn his sword. Had not moved his feet.

"Blood Flame Sect," the Blade Sect Leader said to the corpse. "I told you to stop."

He turned back to Yeon-shin.

"I like you," he said. "That talent. That spirit. You have the makings to reach the pinnacle of the martial world. If you didn't have ties to the Desolate Sect, I would have taken you as a disciple."

Silence. Then the Blade Sect Leader continued.

"I'll accept a duel anytime. Come to take your revenge. I'll be waiting."

Yeon-shin sheathed his sword and turned his back on the most dangerous man he had ever met. He walked toward the main building. His hands trembled at his sides where no one could see them.

*Later. Grieve later. Fall apart later. Not now.*

Behind him, the Blade Sect Leader addressed the Zhongnan Sect masters.

"This land. I no longer permit other sects to enter. I'll give you one day."

A pause. Then Yeo Il-shin's voice, tight with pain and fury:

"We understand."

"Leave."

---

The dead were everywhere.

Yeon-shin walked through the main building and counted them. Guards. Servants. Cousins. Old Zhang the gatekeeper, pipe still clenched between his teeth, chest opened by a single cut.

He found Nam-san and his wife in the corridor outside the children's quarters. Nam-san had placed himself between the attackers and the room where his daughter slept. He had been cut down. His wife had thrown herself over him, and been cut down too.

Yeon-shin knelt. His brother's eyes were open, glazed. He had died fighting.

*You called me a fool every time we spoke. But you died shielding your daughter.*

He pushed the bodies aside.

*Gently.*

The thought surprised him. He had not expected gentleness from himself.

He stamped on the floor. Once, twice, three times. The hidden panel gave way.

A girl lay curled inside. Five years old. Hye-ah, Nam-san's daughter. Unconscious -- her sleep acupoint pressed. Nam-san's last act before he stood in the corridor to die.

*It doesn't matter now.*

He carried Hye-ah out.

Jung-san was in the courtyard. His clothes were unmarked. His sword was clean. When Yeon-shin approached, Jung-san flinched and could not meet his eyes.

Yeon-shin handed him the child.

"Raise her in Zhongnan," he said. "By whatever means necessary."

"You — what about you?"

"I'll be busy looking after myself."

"Raise her in Zhongnan? How could that be possible —"

"Children of five and six become disciples in the Nine Sects. You know this better than I do. Beg if you have to. Her aptitude is better than yours — they'll accept her."

Jung-san bit his lip. Looked down at the unconscious girl in his arms. Something moved in his face — not courage, exactly, but perhaps the faint beginning of responsibility.

*It will have to be enough.*

The Zhongnan Sect masters approached. Yeo Il-shin walked at the front, holding her side, the wound where her ear had been wrapped in a strip of cloth soaked red. Precipice Sword and Shadowless Fist followed, faces grim. They had helped lay the dead in rows. Righteous martial artists. Yeon-shin would remember that.

"Young hero," Yeo Il-shin said.

Not *the family head's youngest son.* Young hero.

"Are you going to the Desolate Sect?"

"I must. There's nowhere else for me to grow stronger."

"The path doesn't only lead to Xiangyang."

Yeon-shin looked at her.

"Come to Mount Zhongnan," she said. "I will recommend you as the sect leader's personal disciple. With your abilities, it is possible."

Silence in the courtyard.

The steward's mouth fell open. Personal disciple of the sect leader. Access to the full Zhongnan sword tradition. A guaranteed path to power and protection.

*She means it.*

"I will not forget your kindness, Senior," Yeon-shin said. He clasped his hands and bowed. "But I must decline."

Yeo Il-shin's face showed resignation, not surprise.

"You are confident," she said. "Confident you can rise higher in the Desolate Sect."

Yeon-shin said nothing.

"I coveted that vessel of yours. The Blade Sect Leader felt the same."

"I'm sorry."

"No need. Zhongnan's doors are always open."

Yeon-shin hesitated. Then:

"Great Elder. You are of advanced age among the noble clans. You have lived centuries. Do you know a way to obtain the fruit of the World Tree?"

The words hung in the air.

Yeo Il-shin's eyes widened. Understanding flooded her face — and with it, something that was almost grief.

"The World Tree's fruit," she whispered. "Then you are —"

"Dying," Yeon-shin said. "Aye."

The word was plain and hard and he said it without flinching.

"I have not lived even half my allotted lifespan," Yeo Il-shin said softly. "I have no authority over the World Tree. Only the Desolate Sect Leader can grant the fruit to another."

"Then I must go to the Desolate Sect."

"It won't be easy. The fruit is a sacred object, even among my people."

"I'll find a way. Rise high enough, earn enough merit, and even sacred objects can be earned."

"I'll support you from afar."

She returned his bow. Her amber eyes were bright with something that looked like grief.

"Well then," she said.

Farewell.

She left first. Precipice Sword and Shadowless Fist would follow the next day, with Jung-san and Hye-ah.

The courtyard emptied. The dead lay in their rows.

*Father.*

Jeong Dae-myeong was alive.

Yeon-shin found him in the reception hall, propped against the wall. The hall was ruined -- scrolls slashed, furniture splintered, the table where they had shared their last meal hacked in two.

Dae-myeong sat against the wall. He was dying. A diagonal slash from hip to ribs, the blood pooled beneath him. His face was the colour of old paper. His breathing shallow, irregular.

On the floor beside him lay the family sword. Green-silk hilt, family crest on the guard. Drawn. The blade was clean.

*He never even got to use it.*

Yeon-shin knelt beside him. He slid one hand behind his father's neck -- the pulse weak, thready. With the other he cradled the back of Dae-myeong's head, holding it the way you hold something that will not survive if you let it fall.

"It's me," he said. "Your third son."

Dae-myeong's eyes opened. Clouded, unfocused. The authority drained from his face, leaving only an old man who was dying. His eyes found Yeon-shin's face. Held.

"Why..." The word was barely audible, a whisper carried on a breath that smelled of blood and bile. "Why are you so strong? How did you..."

The question trailed into a wet cough. Blood at the corner of his mouth, thin and bright.

*If you were going to die like this, you should have treated me better.*

The thought was there, hot and bitter. He could say it. A small revenge for fifteen years of coldness.

He did not say it.

"What I created and mastered," Yeon-shin said instead, "I call it the Jeong Family Dynamic Technique."

He watched his father's face.

Something changed in his father's eyes. A light -- dim, guttering, but real.

"Jeong Family... Dynamic Technique."

A pause. The breathing hitched. The rigid lines of his father's face loosened, fell away, and beneath them was something raw and heartbroken. A face Yeon-shin had never seen before.

"A divine skill," Dae-myeong whispered. "Created by a Jeong. This foolish and ignorant... Jeong Dae-myeong. The son I bore. The third son of an incompetent family head... created a divine skill."

*He is proud of me.*

Yeon-shin's jaw clamped shut. His eyes burned -- not the crown gate. Tears he would not permit to fall.

"You have," Dae-myeong said.

His hand moved. Slowly, with terrible effort, he raised it from the floor and placed it on Yeon-shin's forearm. The fingers were cold and slick with blood. But the grip was deliberate. A father touching his son.

For the first time.

"The qualities."

The grip tightened. One last contraction of muscle and will.

"Of a great master."

The hand fell.

The light behind his eyes was gone. The breathing had stopped. The body was just a body.

Yeon-shin knelt with his father's head in his hand. Something hot ran down his cheek. He wiped it away with the back of his blood-crusted hand.

Not one sound.

He laid his father's head down gently. Closed the open eyes with two fingers, the way he had seen the Zhongnan masters do for the other dead. Straightened the ruined robes. Folded the cold hands across the wound in the belly, hiding it.

Then he picked up the family sword. Good steel. The green-silk hilt was warm. He slid the blade into his own belt.

*Grandfather's sword. Father's sword.*

Something in his chest eased. Not the grief. The loneliness.

He stood. Walked to the door. In the east, the first grey light of dawn.

He had five years.

He would make them count.
